name: Baker, Arthur Adolphus
description: Behold the "Prince of Knockers," destined to excel Elbert Hubbard, who has nothing but a little experience on Bake when it comes to wielding the good old hammer. His monthly pamphlet, the Pennant, might well be mistaken for the Philistine were it not for the difference in binding; and few are the college organizations that have escaped unscathed. Up to date but one thing has come into this old world that has measured up to Bake's high ideals, and that is Arthur A. Baker. Lest he should show partiality and give any bunch of Profs. the joy of having too excellent a student under them, Bake has religiously changed his course each year, with the result that the "Ags" win; for they were his last hope and, therefore, the ones favored in his senior year. If we were called on to describe him in one word, it could well be done by the word lazy. However, when all is said and done Bake is all there, one of the best men that ever represented the Orange and Blue on the grid-iron, strong with the student-body and a corking good scout.

name: Bamberg, Charles Sheldon
major: Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering
description: An electrical engineer of small stature but with a considerable amount of gray-matter, quiet and unobtrusive, but one upon whom you can count. He is ever pursuing the knowledge that trickles from "Iky's" fount, and as a result by this time is a brilliant performer on the "slide rule." He came to us many, many years ago, and ever since has grown steadily in "sophistication" and knowledge, but not in size. Bam is "around here" when it comes to the ladies; most any night you may see him mounting his wheel (by the way, did you ever see anyone else mounting said wheel?) to ride away and make new conquests.

name: Bryant, Thomas W.
clubs: Board of Control Medal in Oratory in Junior Class, 1911
description: Here you see one of Florida's foremost politicians of the future, the silver-tongued orator of the College, a past and present master in the noble art of "Bull," always ready with a line that is convincing, no matter what the subject or how little his knowledge upon it. Tommie, as his record will show, has held many important positions here. He is popular, despite the fact that he is a man of decided likes and dislikes, which he does not try to conceal. You have probably heard him express his opinion of a certain Prof. in language which has often taxed even his splendid supply of endearing (?) terms (which is "going some"); but after several years of vain endeavor, he has passed said Prof., and his "dip" is secure.

name: Brassell, Clarence J.
major: Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering
description: "Brazil" blew in here from the Alabama Polytechnic Institute in 1911. The why of anything he never explains, and we all suppose that he is looking after his own affairs, if he has any. If any of us know "Brazil," it has not been found out by any of the rest of us. He is an unknown quantity, but we hope to know something about him when he becomes an engineer. The city from which "Brazil" comes has furnished us a variegated lot of specimens, but this one we are unable to classify. Alabama was too cold for him, so he returned to Florida, and will probably end his days monkeying with electric lights in Tampa.

name: Davis, Albert G.
description: The original Sphinx, and one who is most skilled in keeping profound and learned silence. Probably the biggest high-brow in the college when he is not speaking, and one who has the faculty bluffed to a cold fade-away. His specialty is Chemistry, and when Dr. Flint's lectures become too abstruse and vague, he gives special classes, explaining them to the unsuspecting "freshies." "A. G." has a bright future ahead of him in whatever line he may choose, as he can stall through any situation and come out on top. He was at his zenith during his junior year, when he rose to the dizzy and dazzling pinnacle of fame as major of the Battalion, and right well did he carry off that honor. While not a member of the Dramatic Club, he is strong on dramatic talent. With the assistance of Dr. Chapman, he staged a show that played to a S. R. O. house, and those who were fortunate enough to be among "those present" will remember him long by it.

name: Felton, Thomas David
major: Bachelor of Science in Civil Engineering
clubs: Honorable mention for Buckman Medal, 1909 and 1911
Editor Y. M. C. A. Handbook, 1911
description: We have all seen "dummies," and heard them not talk, but here we have a man differing from them only in the fact that he does not even make signs; for great indeed is the event that will cause a word to drop from his lips unless perchance you are one of the "chosen few." Ever quiet and reserved but with the biggest of hearts, ready to do any man a good turn, "Dave" is one of the most popular men in college. For three long, weary years he pursued ever-fleeting wisdom with no other nickname than "T. D." or "Dave"; but doubtless, wearying of these, he resolved to annex another and more fitting pseudonym in his fourth year, and right well did he "turn the trick" on last Thanksgiving night. It was thusly—but possibly you had better get "Philpott" himself or Howze, his valet, to tell the tale.

name: Grace, George J.
description: Grace hails from Graceville, so named, we are told, because he was the discoverer of that far-famed metropolis. Up to the present time but few others have ventured that far into the wilderness. Grace is firstly and foremostly a student, and in that role he shines, for he is a firm believer in consuming the "midnight-electricity." When it comes to Principles of Education he is there with both feet, and then some, and can learnedly discuss any phase of the subject yet invented. He is of a sedate and retiring disposition, old beyond his years, but one who will doubtless come to the front as an educator of no mean ability.

name: Jacobson, Joseph E.
description: One of the "Old Guard" who was present at the founding of our university, and has been here ever since, save during a short vacation during his sophomore year. At last he appears in his cap-and-gown, with the same old smile worn in his characteristic way. But say, did you hear him "orate" last commencement? If not you missed a treat. However, he will gladly go over it for you at any time, with reasons appended as to why he did not win the medal. It is a sad story, and runs thusly: "Tommie won the medal, for he roomed with 'Bake' and 'Bake's' brother was one of the judges." It was a foregone conclusion that Tommie would win; for what flaw could one possibly find in that reasoning? But "Jakie" is a good scout; he will not assay one ounce of tight-wad to the ton.

name: Kime, Charles Davidson
description: Herewith you see portrayed a man without a nickname, a sad calamity indeed; but notwithstanding this great handicap, Kime is quite popular with those who know him. A future tiller of the soil, though you would never suspect it, nor does he advertise the fact. He comes to us from the University of Michigan, though once upon a time, long ago, he attended Florida and the 'Call of the Wild' was too great, and now again we have him with us, a sadder but a wiser youth. In those days he was a perfect lady, but alas, he too has backslidden, and in case of necessity can dissipate wildly; in fact, he has been seen smoking a cigarette, and once when he was deeply stirred, was heard to mutter "darn". At Michigan he did the "440," as we have since been informed, and his one regret is that Florida does not put forth a track team; for there he stars.

name: Miller, Oscar Samuel
motto: "What in me is dark illumine; what is low raise and support."
description: Migrated from Leland Stanford Junior University in 1911.

name: Perkins, Lawrence Anderson Jr.
clubs: Secretary and Treasurer of the Class, 1912
description: Destined for the stage and slated for the pulpit, we have here a man of many talents, but these do not run towards anything that would make him stir faster than is his wont to go. By countless years of practice, "Perk" has become the most capable and efficient "Trotter" in college, and can fluently translate any Latin or Greek in captivity,— from the "Jack,"—but to hear him struggling helplessly in the classroom, would move the hardest-hearted statue to tears; however "Andy," alas, is impregnable. "A Lady Fusser" of great ability, self-confessed, due chiefly to his fair complexion and pretty form. It is rumored that due to the necessities of the stage, "Perk" is going to soil his heretofore unkissed lips with Lady Nicotine, but you can judge from his picture whether or not this rumor be true.

name: Shackleford, Robert Wooten
description: From a small boy "Shack" has developed all at the same time into a heavy-weight student; a middle weight ladies' man; and though still a light-weight in football, he has made an All-Florida quarter-back. "Shack," though at times his flaming complexion has threatened it, has never quite "set the world on fire." However, he is liked by the men that know him, and already shows signs of having chosen a life's vocation; so with this early start and the salutary circumstance accompanying it, some day we should not be surprised to hear of him as the head of the house of Bloomstein. But if tailoring fail him, or he never advance further than the flat-iron stage of the industry, we are reassured of his ultimate success in life by the fact that he contemplates fitting himself for the law.

name: Storter, Neal S.
major: Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering
description: Hails from the Everglades, but has been reclaimed, and after years of draining, has almost entirely lost his old-time Evergladian mannerisms. "Bo's" most prominent feature is the ever-present "F" sweater, from which he has never been known to be separated, during winter or summer, on football field or at social function. Having succeeded in getting into nearly every other phase of college life, "Bo" is now trying out as a social butterfly, and seems to be making good. He is one of the most influential and popular men in college, something of a demagogue, but destined to make a success; not as an engineer, though that is his present course, but as a politician; for he is ever there with a good word for everyone. As a four-year star on the gridiron, and Captain of the 1911 team, he is best known; and right well has he battled for the Orange and Blue.

name: Thomas, Daniel Frazier
major: Bachelor of Science in Civil Engineering
description: Gab! Why, he is the gabbiest man you ever saw, his favorite expression being, "Go to and stay put." Frazier is a hail-fellow-well-met, our only fear being that he will cause some poor soul heart failure, for if you give him your attention long enough he will surely talk you to death. He is some strong with the ladies, too, a fact which seems strange, as they cannot possibly get in a word edgewise when with him, which is not to the liking of most of them. He is fond of taking trips to Ocala. Why? We will not say. He is taking thirty-two hours a week in order to graduate with the rest of the class, and judging from the fact that he has taken up his abode in the laboratories, he bids fair to get through.

name: Buie, Dugal Mccree
description: The record of this "Tar-Baby" up to date comprises academic work at Davidson, some law at Chapel Hill, two years at the University of Florida, and a special course in Domestic Relations at Waldo. His reticence is due to the fact that he is a deaf-mute. Despite these disabling and deplorable vocal deficiencies, "Doug" is the dinkiest deity of the diamond, the crowned king of the third sack. Though he has loitered leisurely along the highways of learning, plucking a flower here and chasing a butterfly there, the Dean will tell you that "Mr. Buie" will arrive "festinatio legis est noverca infortunii."

name: Cason, Fred Warren
motto: "You will find a heart of truth within a rough outside."
description: With a sole ambition to become a great lawyer and statesman, Fred loses not a single moment, but employs his time systematically and proportionately, according to their relative importance, with law books, newspapers, magazines, tennis, chess, yarn-spinning; especially to the last occupation is he a noted devotee. He does not approve of throwing away valuable time with the ladies, thus evidencing an aspiration for something higher than the position of a mere social pedant, or signs of a heart, broken and disappointed in the past.

name: DeVane, Enoch Clabe
description: "Big Andy" began his law course at Washington and Lee University two years ago, but being rather slow in asserting himself, he was three long months in deciding that the climate was unsuited to a South Florida Alligator. Having thus discovered that his feet were cold, he placed them in the fire here, where they have been blistering ever since. He is twenty-two years old, mild tempered, congenial, sociable, self-doubting, but possessor of more friends than he realizes. He was desirous of being one of the Florida Football-Bear-Cats, but having played on the Washington and Lee team last year, was disqualified.

name: Donnell, Elza Ballard
clubs: Principal of High School, Abbeville and St. Martinsville, La.
City School Superintendent, Baton Rouge, La.
description: "Don" stands high in his classes—about 9 ft., 14 in.—would not stoop to a little thing because it would take too long to right himself. That hen-pecked expression was not caused by any domestic infelicity, but is the result of attempting to teach irrational quantities to still more irrational sub-freshmen. His favorite occupations are going to church and raising poultry—chicken pi-e-ty, so to speak. Excellent in classwork, and a large and luminous star in Practice Court. He expects to devote the remainder of his life to teaching law to the ignorant members of the bar at Jacksonville.

name: Ferrell, Henry Archer
clubs: Ex-Principal Chapman High School, Fernandina High School, Jefferson Collegiate Institute
Instructor Summer Training School for Teachers, Tallahassee and Gainesville, 1907-1910
Author "Government of Florida," adopted by State Uniform Text Book Commission
description: We have at great expense of life and limb captured this wild soaring eagle of the wind-tipped clouds, and show him now for the first time caged in the confines of this book. "All his triumphs, strategems, and spoils sunk to this little measure." Here is the strangest mixture of heterogenous elements that ever seethed and battled within the soul of man. His ebolitions vary from the harmless vaporings of a discredited demagogue to the ominous roars of a Numidian lion. Author, Poet, Politician, Teacher, Orator, and Bull-Artist; with a touch of youthful fire and enthusiasm rarely seen in one who has felt so extensively the bludgeonings of the commercial world. Give him a big chew of tobacco, then suggest the subject of confederate tradition, and from A to Z he will proceed to justify beyond all possibility of attack any contention that the honorable old veteran ever supported.

name: Fields, William Preston
motto: "I am a natural born farmer, An agriculturist by trade, But pretty soon I'll take up law, And lay the plow in the shade."
description: Know all ye whom it may concern, that you are indebted to the back woods of Liberty County, which were so liberal in giving up their last twenty-six years' production, in order to present to us a spectacular exhibit of a West Florida Field. True and faithful to the core. Just as he that sitteth on a hot stove shall rise, so shall he that tramples upon this Field fall.

name: Harrison, William Thomas
description: This package of plain jewels originated in Alachua County, but the latter should not be held responsible, for Manatee did the training. Born and raised on the farm, but believing that it would be an irreparable loss to the State for him to continue such a trade, he now branches out into a broader sphere. Old Harrison is teeming over with dry jokes, all of which are taken from his experience on the farm. Here's hoping that his dreams of success will come true, and that the law office will be as satisfactory as the art of milking goats.

name: Sherod, Sheffield Keen, Jr.
description: With a head determined to acquire an LL.B. degree, this "Keen-Cutter" has been devoting all the time he could spare from courtship to the study of law. In the former subject he has succeeded so admirably that in the future we expect great things from him in the latter. For quite a long time he has been building air-castles upon the possibility of his becoming a lawyer, but now that this possibility has become a certainty, we feel confident that he will anchor them safely to earth.

name: King, Roswell
description: "Shorty" is small of stature and deceiving in looks. He is accused of possessing a superabundance of executive ability. He does not believe in much work, but boastfully and successfully makes the bystanders think his time well employed by "previous engagements." "Happy", another characteristic name—especially appropriate when a trip to Jacksonville is in view,—is more fond of town than the campus. While on the street he informs the gossipers that he has a business appointment around the corner; so around the corner he goes, with his bow legs and extreme parrot toes. Without pausing a moment, he lands the same report on that side of the block, and with acceleration, beats his way on around the corner to the starting point. Thus he spends his time while not at work, and thereby holds his friends at such a distance that they have no opportunity to become tired of, or disappointed in him. With such cute and cunning ways, he not only commands the respect of the students, but has fooled the Dean into having a good opinion of him.

name: Bascom, Vernanda Mathis
description: Age, twenty-two; long, lean, and lanky; born and bred in the State of Florida; Bascom portrays the motto, "Appropriate silence presents an unknown quantity." With his slow start and steady pace, Bascom will reach the goal on the last day of grace. He served the young Americans as a teacher until 1910, when, deciding that he had missed his calling, he removed to the University of Florida to take up the study of law. But now his nature yearns for a new calling and we find him forsaking the law for a commercial career.

name: McMullen, Melvin Arthur
description: He is one of those fellows that believes in the old saying, that once you have tried and have not succeeded, try, try again. He entered the law department of Washington and Lee University in 1906, but owing to the attractiveness of a fair, blue-eyed Miss, whom he had left behind, Old Mac found that he was unable to say, "Wait till the sun shines, Nellie, and the college years have drifted by," so he returned to his attraction and said, "We will now be happy, Nellie, you and I." Mac's middle name should have been Indomitable Perseverance; for no sooner had he kissed them all good-bye at Washington and Lee than he gave the glad "howdy-do" to the Senior Law men of Florida, bringing with him the aforementioned attraction—plus.

name: Mershon, Martin Luther
description: It reads like the wanderings of Ulysses or the biography of Homer himself: St. Mary's, Fitzgerald, and Douglas in the State of Georgia, Kissimmee, Tampa, Plant City, Fernandina, and Gainesville in Florida,—all these cities and near-villages claim and counterclaim this illustrious son of an illustrious sire as their own. Illustrious, not industrious, Terese, was the word we used. Reared in an atmosphere of legal lore, he wrote a learned treatise on "Dilatory Pleas" at the age of seven, but in later years he has turned his attention to "Burdick on Torts", particularly the "case work." Modest; spendthrift; critic but champion of his friends; aristocrat without snobbishness; a lazy racehorse, but under the whip, a cyclonic thoroughbred. Mershon can—and easily. Will he?

name: Moon, Charles Morris
clubs: Secretary and Treasurer, John Marshall Debating Society
description: Two falls ago brought to the environs of the campus a bright "Moon", veiled in such glittering apparel that the eyes of all were drawn upon him and deprived of their ability to detect his imperfections. He winked his eye, gave a broad smile, and with an ever-moving, ever-increasing ebullition of hot-air, caused the ladies to dream of air-castles, and his fellow students to stand aside and gaze on in admiration. But then came the wily Dean, and with his deft hand and skilled probe, reached behind the mysterious light to test its genuineness. It led him on like the Will-o'-the-Wisp, now plainly visible, now transpiring, uncertain: until at last it betrayed its vulnerable spot: "Mr. Moon, will you think before you speak, and kindly make yourself clear?" Charlie has a good business head, and we expect him to make a good attorney.

name: Martin Luther O'Berry
description: This "Berry" came to us from Columbia College. He is twenty-one years old, and would be spoiled if it were not for the professors constantly throwing hot spices in his path. He is said to have a good head, but if he has, he sometimes appears to lack the energy to use it. Give him a pretty horse and buggy with a fair damsel, or a comfortable sleeping-chair in class, and to him this world is a paradise.

name: Aurelius, Edwin Philips
description: Thus hails to the University of his native State, Edwin, after having obtained his B. A. and Girlology degrees at the University of Tennessee. He is a business man by instinct, and a love-maker by constant experience; the latter imputation he denies because he never sees a girl oftener than seven days out of the week. He is usually top-notch in his law classes, but when not so, you may note that he is becoming more proficient in Girlology. A man of commanding appearance; at first sight you would think him a dignified, garrulous, and unsociable book-worm, but his friendship, like whisky, grows stronger with age.

name: Price, William Clinton
clubs: Secretary and Treasurer Senior Law Class
description: "Clint" divides his time between law, tennis, bridge-whist, and mess-hall. He is always on time, especially at mess-hall. He has an abnormal propensity for avoiding barbers, but whether this grows out of natural laziness or an exaggerated idea of the beauty of long curls, we are unable to ascertain. When quizzed by the Profs., he always states that he does not know what the book says, but his recitation discloses the fact that he has had a remarkably long line of personal experience for so young a lad, or else exemplifies that aged maxim that "great minds run in the same channel."

name: Redden, Wallace Randell
description: Above this epitaph we see A face of solemn mystery, The eyes inscrutable, concealing Both passion fierce and kindly feeling. Though meager this memorial, His figure dictatorial, In owlish grandeur, sits and blinks, And gives the idea that he thinks; But we who all his actions know Perceive that this is done for show: Although in law a mere apprentice, Some interval of compos mentis, Perchance, may o'er his features steal, And all his wilder deeds repeal. Though not averse to criticism, He would be first in witticism; As small fires often cause much smoke, His laughter far out-weighs his joke. Of dances, and the ladies fond, Our bold "ex-judge" and "weak-eyed blond."

name: Trantham, Thomas Simmons
clubs: Secretary to the President of the University, 1910-12
description: Hailed to our State some years ago, a rough and timid country youth, from Camden, S. C. With a strong mental and physical constitution for a foundation, and "Success" graven on his brow as a motto, he came to prepare himself for paddling life's canoe. Here's hoping that opportunity will not find him asleep, as does mess-hall breakfast. Procrastination and forgetfulness have become his predominant characteristics; these coupled with his frequent calls to Tampa, might suggest to the minds of the romantic a less prosaic explanation of these visits than he gives, to wit: "business." However, we will leave to time the task of analyzing his condition.

name: Walker, William Stanton
clubs: Attorney General John Marshall Debating Society, 1910-11
description: "Doc" inherited his nick-name. Prior to the Pure Food and Drugs Act of 1906, this label was sufficient. Now, however, in the interest of the public at large, for the prevention of fraud and deceit, we are admonished to change it so as to comply with the requirements of either of the following analyses: (1) "Clemson" 50 per cent, "South Carolina" 40 per cent, "Virginia" 5 per cent, "Florida" 5 per cent; (2) "Democracy" 1 per cent, "Athletics" 1 per cent, "Athletic heart" 98 per cent; but being unable to find a name sufficiently comprehensive to embody all these elements, we have decided to call him "It," and depend upon the various ingredients to manifest themselves. Well, we are told that "Doc," believing, like Wordsworth and Coleridge, that nature is the source of all knowledge, sought learning on a lonely sun-kissed peak in Virginia. This method failing him, he tried the method of "inspiration," until Dean interrupted his sweet, slumberous dream with the harsh yet romantic remark that, "The law is a jealous mistress." Since this awakening "Doc" has stuck to "mother-earth," "brass-tacks," and his law books with a zeal that plainly spells "Success."
